items.text: The draft resolution of the Working Committee is based on Ahimsa, the contemplated struggle similarly has its roots in Ahimsa. Here is a mantra, a short one that I give you. You may imprint it on your hearts and let every breath of yours give expression to it. The mantra is: 'Do or Die'. We shall either free India or die in the attempt; we shall not live to see the perpetuation of our slavery.
items.context: Delivered to the All-India Congress Committee (A.I.C.C.) in Bombay on August 8, 1942, introducing the Quit India Movement and the non-violent satyagraha campaign
items.publicReaction: Within less than twenty-four hours, Gandhi and almost the entire Indian National Congress leadership were imprisoned by the British under the Defense of India Act. The 'Do or Die' mantra became the unifying rallying cry for civil disobedience that spread across India from August 1942 to September 1944.
items.source: Speeches and Writings of M. K. Gandhi; mkgandhi.org; The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi Vol 45

items.text: In all probability this will be my last speech to you. Even if the Government allow me to march tomorrow morning, this will be my last speech on the sacred banks of the Sabarmati. Possibly these may be the last words of my life here. You may take it as my will... I wish that there should be no suspension or abandonment of the war that commences tomorrow morning... I have faith in the righteousness of our cause and the purity of our weapons. And where the means are clean, there God is undoubtedly present with His blessings. A Satyagrahi, whether free or incarcerated, is ever victorious.
items.context: Delivered at the evening prayer on the Sabarmati sands in Ahmedabad on March 11, 1930, on the eve of his march to Dandi to break the salt laws. A crowd of 10,000 gathered at this prayer meeting.
items.publicReaction: The speech electrified the independence movement. Gandhi was arrested on May 5, 1930, after reaching Dandi. The Salt March became a pivotal moment in India's independence struggle and inspired civil disobedience movements worldwide.
items.source: Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi, Volume Six; Gandhi Ashram Sabarmati archives

items.text: I have become an uncompromising disaffectionist and non-co-operator. Non-violence is the first article of my faith. It is also the last article of my creed. I am here, therefore, to invite and cheerfully submit to the highest penalty that can be inflicted upon me for what in law is a deliberate crime, and what appears to me to be the highest duty of a citizen. Non-co-operation with evil is as much a duty as is co-operation with good.
items.context: Statement made during the Great Trial on March 18, 1922, in Ahmedabad before Judge C. N. Broomfield. Gandhi was charged under Section 124 A of the Indian Penal Code for sedition based on three articles published in Young India: 'Tampering with Loyalty,' 'The Puzzle and its Solution,' and 'Shaking the Manes.'
items.publicReaction: Gandhi pleaded guilty and requested the severest penalty. He was sentenced to six years imprisonment. The trial and his dignified statement gained international attention and strengthened his moral authority in the independence movement.
items.source: Great Trial of 1922 statement; mkgandhi.org; The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi

items.text: I am deeply sorry for it and I am, therefore, here to submit not to a light penalty but to the highest penalty... The only course open to you, the Judge and the assessors, is either to resign your posts and thus dissociate yourselves from evil, if you feel that the law you are called upon to administer is an evil, or to inflict on me the severest penalty, if you believe that the system and the law you are assisting to administer are good for the people of this country.
items.context: From Gandhi's written statement presented and read aloud during the Great Trial of 1922. This portion addresses the court directly about the moral choice before the judge and assessors.
items.publicReaction: His eloquent invocation of conscience in the courtroom was widely reported in Indian and international newspapers, further establishing Gandhi's status as a principled leader willing to sacrifice for his convictions.
items.source: Great Trial of 1922 statement; The Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi; Gandhi Ashram Sabarmati archives

items.text: Spiritual life... can be transmitted through the lip is mistaken. There is no salvation for India unless you strip yourselves of this jewellery and this gold. The fact is that you cannot serve the creator while serving mammon... there is no salvation for India unless you strip yourselves of this jewellery. I have not come here to flatter you. You are gaily bedecked in jewels and silk... You are living in an atmosphere where you do not even think of the poor people of India.
items.context: Delivered on February 4, 1916, at the inaugural ceremony of Banaras Hindu University, attended by the Viceroy Lord Hardinge and numerous princes and nobility. This was Gandhi's first public address in India, challenging the elite's ostentatious display of wealth amid Indian poverty.
items.publicReaction: The speech was interrupted by platform movements and objections from Mrs. Besant. It shocked the wealthy audience but resonated powerfully with the broader Indian public, establishing Gandhi as a voice speaking for the poor and dispossessed.
items.source: Speeches and Writings of M. K. Gandhi; Wikisource; Gandhi-Manibhavan archives

items.text: Before embarking on Civil Disobedience and taking the risk I have dreaded to take all these years, I would fain approach you and find a way out. I have nothing to fear. I am not angry with you. I am anxious for your co-operation... You are today the one person in the world who can prevent a war which may reduce humanity to a savage state.
items.context: Letter written to the Viceroy Lord Irwin on March 2, 1930, giving advance notice of the planned Salt March and civil disobedience campaign beginning March 11, 1930. Gandhi gave the Viceroy nine days' notice before breaking the Salt Laws.
items.publicReaction: The Viceroy did not respond directly but expressed through his secretary his regret that Gandhi would resort to breaking the law. Gandhi began the Satyagraha from Sabarmati Ashram on March 12, 1930, with hundreds of followers, sparking the major civil disobedience movement.
items.source: The Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi; Government of India archives; Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi

items.text: I would fain approach you and find a way out... Bones have been broken, private parts have been squeezed for the purpose of making volunteers give up, to the Government valueless, to the volunteers precious salt. I have not two ways about it. Either the salt tax must be removed and private salt-making allowed to be carried on so that every person in India can manufacture salt in his own home... or I will be compelled to lead a civil disobedience.
items.context: From Gandhi's letter to Lord Irwin on May 4, 1930, written after his arrest and presented by his followers. This letter documents government violence against salt satyagrahis and outlines Gandhi's demands before escalating the campaign.
items.publicReaction: The letter's documentation of brutality strengthened international sympathy for the independence movement and exposed British repression to world opinion.
items.source: The Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi; The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi

items.text: I write to you to appeal to your heart. Friends have been urging me to write to you for the sake of humanity. But I have resisted their request, because of the feeling that any letter from me would be an impertinence... It is quite clear that you are today the one person in the world who can prevent a war which may reduce humanity to the savage state... Will you listen to the appeal of one who has deliberately shunned the method of war not without considerable success?
items.context: Letter written to Adolf Hitler on July 23, 1939, just weeks before Nazi Germany's invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939), which triggered World War II. Gandhi wrote to Hitler as a last appeal for peace through non-violence.
items.publicReaction: The letter was prevented from reaching Hitler by the British government. There is no evidence Hitler ever responded. Gandhi wrote again in 1940 after the war began, but with no documented effect on Nazi policy.
items.source: The Selected Letters of Mahatma Gandhi; mkgandhi.org; Time magazine archives

items.text: This quarrel is not old; this quarrel is coeval with the British Advent. I came to the conclusion long ago that all religions were true and that also that all had some error in them, and while I hold by my own religion, I should hold other religions as dear as Hinduism. The British have divided Hindus and Muslims. Once India achieves independence, different religious communities will coexist harmoniously as they did before colonialism.
items.context: Address delivered at the Round Table Conference in London on November 30, 1931. Gandhi spoke as the sole representative of the Indian National Congress, advocating for complete independence and religious unity.
items.publicReaction: The speech was received as a powerful articulation of India's aspirations for independence and unity. It challenged British colonial narratives and strengthened Gandhi's international standing as a spokesperson for Indian nationalism and interfaith harmony.
items.source: Speech at the Round Table Conference; mkgandhi.org; Gandhi Ashram Sevagram archives

items.text: It is Swaraj when we learn to rule ourselves. It is, therefore, in the palm of our hands. The force of love is the same as the force of the soul or truth. Passive resistance is a method of securing rights by personal suffering; it is the reverse of resistance by arms. The English have not taken India; we have given it to them through internal divisions and cooperation with colonial commerce.
items.context: From Hind Swaraj (Indian Home Rule), written by Gandhi in Gujarati between November 13-22, 1909, aboard the ship Kildonan Castle during his return voyage from London to South Africa. The work is structured as a dialogue between two characters.
items.publicReaction: The book was banned by the British government in India in 1910 as seditious. It became a foundational text for the independence movement and Gandhi's philosophy of self-rule and non-violence, and remains influential today.
items.source: Hind Swaraj or Indian Home Rule; Government of India publications; Internet Archive; Wikisource

items.text: I have become an uncompromising disaffectionist towards the existing system of Government. India is less manly under the British rule than she ever was before. In nine out of every ten cases, condemned men were totally innocent. I wanted to avoid violence. Non-violence is the first article of my faith.
items.context: From Gandhi's written statement in the Great Trial of 1922, where he detailed his political evolution from a loyalist to an opponent of British rule, explaining the turning points including the Rowlatt Act, Jallianwala Bagh massacre, and broken promises on the Khilafat.
items.publicReaction: The statement's frank acknowledgment of violence while maintaining commitment to non-violence generated international discussion about Gandhi's moral philosophy and the justifiability of civil disobedience against unjust rule.
items.source: Great Trial of 1922 statement; The Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi; mkgandhi.org

items.text: Study this spinning-wheel of mine. It would teach you a great deal more than I can—patience, industry, simplicity. This spinning-wheel is for India's starving millions the symbol of salvation. The charkha is the symbol for non-violence on which all life, if it is to be real life, must be based.
items.context: Statement about the Charkha (spinning wheel) made in The Daily Herald, London on September 28, 1931, during Gandhi's visit to Britain. Also documented in Harijan on April 27, 1947. The spinning wheel was central to Gandhi's Swadeshi (indigenous self-reliance) program.
items.publicReaction: The statement promoted handspinning as a practical and symbolic act of independence. The Charkha became an iconic symbol of the Indian independence movement and appeared on the Indian flag.
items.source: The Daily Herald, London; Harijan publication; Gandhi Ashram Sevagram archives

items.text: I came to the conclusion long ago that all religions were true and that also that all had some error in them, and while I hold by my own religion, I should hold other religions as dear as Hinduism.
items.context: Statement from Gandhi's writings on religious philosophy and interfaith harmony, documented in the collection 'All Men Are Brothers' (p. 57).
items.publicReaction: This nuanced articulation of religious pluralism became central to Gandhi's public identity as an advocate of communal harmony. It influenced interfaith dialogue and inspired leaders like Martin Luther King Jr.
items.source: 'All Men Are Brothers' collection (A&C Black); The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi

items.context: Statement made in response to requests for messages to foreign audiences. Documented in interviews from 1942 onwards. In 1942, Karl Quimby recorded Gandhi saying this when asked for a message to Americans. In 1945, journalist Deton J. Brooks of 'The Chicago Defender' interviewed Gandhi in India, and Gandhi wrote on a note pad: 'My life is its own message.'
items.publicReaction: The phrase became widely quoted in Gandhi's lifetime and has become one of his most recognized statements, used to encapsulate his philosophy that actions speak louder than words. However, scholars note the phrase appears in earlier sources (Charles E. Garst, 1898).
items.source: Quote Investigator archive; Karl Quimby 1942; Deton J. Brooks Chicago Defender interview 1945; Press Information Bureau of India

items.text: The draft resolution of the Working Committee is based on Ahimsa. I wanted to avoid violence. Non-violence is the first article of my faith. I do not ask for mercy. I do not plead any extenuating act.
items.context: From Gandhi's oral statement at the opening of the Great Trial of 1922. He acknowledged responsibility for violence that erupted during the independence movement, including the Chauri Chaura incident, while reaffirming his core commitment to non-violence.
items.publicReaction: His willingness to accept responsibility while maintaining moral consistency impressed observers internationally and reinforced his image as a principled leader.
items.source: Great Trial of 1922; The Selected Works of Mahatma Gandhi; mkgandhi.org

items.context: Gandhi's last words spoken on January 30, 1948, at approximately 5:13 PM, as he emerged from Birla House to walk to the evening prayer meeting in the garden. He was shot three times at point-blank range by Nathuram Godse.
items.publicReaction: The words resonated globally as Gandhi's final testimony to his lifelong commitment to divine faith and non-violence, even in his moment of death. His assassination shocked the world and elevated his status as a martyr for peace.
items.source: Eyewitness accounts; biographies by Lou Fischer, Ramachandra Guha, and others; UCLA MANAS South Asia archives

items.text: Because he was an 'Untouchable by adoption', he was 'more of an Untouchable in mind' than Ambedkar. However, in the course of that very conversation, Gandhi referred to the Untouchables as 'your community', and made a strong plea to Ambedkar on behalf of 'my Caste Hindu brethren'. Most of my time goes in reading Gandhiji's untouchability-eradication statements. Gandhiji should now be called 'our man', because he is now speaking our language and our thoughts.
items.context: From the negotiations leading to the Poona Pact on September 24, 1932. Gandhi met with B.R. Ambedkar to negotiate representation for depressed classes (Scheduled Castes) in the legislature. The quote mixes Gandhi's statements from September 22 and Ambedkar's written assessment from November 14, 1932, to Janata readers.
items.publicReaction: The Pact increased reserved seats from 71 to 148 for depressed classes. While praised by some, it remained controversial because it did not grant separate electorates (which Ambedkar demanded). Gandhi broke his fast on September 26, 1932, following the agreement.
items.source: Poona Pact documentation; Constitution of India archives; B.R. Ambedkar writings in Janata
